coredata.dc:title: K+ concentration-based NMR-fluorescence dual-functional senescence sensing using graphene quantum dots with crown ether structure

Tao, Quan

Rong, Liangliang
coredata.dc:description: Aging allows the occurrence and progression of cerebrovascular diseases, highlighting the importance of detecting senescent cells and tissues in the nervous system. Detecting senescent cells/tissues using nanoparticles as probes in the nervous system is desired with high sensitivity, high accuracy, and penetration of the blood-brain barrier. In response, we designed crown ether-based graphene quantum dots with fast proton transport channels to sense potassium ions, a significant differential indicator between healthy and senescent cells. Using the probe, we have achieved MRI-fluorescence dual-functional monitoring of potassium ion concentration in cells via fluorescent intensity and magnetic relaxation time. Importantly, we have further demonstrated the advantages of the crown ether-based graphene quantum dots in penetrating the blood-brain barrier, revealing their potential for diagnosing aging in the nervous system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-function
Ultra-low-field (ULF) MRI
Magnetic relaxation sensing
Fluorescent sensing
Graphene quantum dots
Cerebrovascular diseases

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of K+ responsive crown ether-structured GQDs (ce-GQDs)
Synthesis of Gd3+ loaded PEG5 modified ce-GQDs
Fluorescent sensing mechanism of ce-GQDs towards K+
Relaxometry sensing mechanism of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ towards K+
Biological application of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ in vitro and in vivo

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Dr. Yongqiang Li from SIMIT for helpful discussion. All animal experiments were conducted following protocols approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the Xinhua Hospital Ethics Committee affiliated to Shanghai Jiaotong University School of Medicine. This work was financially supported by International Partnership Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (321GJHZ2023246GC), Autonomous Deployment Project of National Key Laboratory of Materials for Integrated Circuits (NKLJC-Z2023-B03), Chinese Natural Science Foundation (82100426). Prof. Siwei Yang acknowledges the support from the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of Chinese Academy of Sciences, and the Xinweizhixing Project of Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of ce-GQDs. (A) Synthesis strategy for K+-fluorescence responsive ce-GQDs (EtOH: ethyl alcohol). (B) TEM image of ce-GQDs (Inset: distribution histogram of ce-GQDs in lateral size, scale bar: 20 nm). (C) High-resolution TEM image of a single ce-GQD particle. (Highlighting the crown ether structure within the yellow cutline, scale bar: 0.2 nm) (D) AFM topography image of ce-GQDs (scale bar: 2 μm). (E) Height profile analysis along the lines shown in (D). (F) XPS C 1 s spectrum. (G) XPS O 1 s spectrum of ce-GQDs.

The fluorescent sensing mechanism of ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion towards K+. (A) The fluorescent quenching sensing mechanism of ce-GQDs with the presence of K+. (B) PL, PLE, and the UV–vis absorption spectra of ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion. (C) Anti-interference study of ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion with the presence of ions or anions in organisms (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, NH4 +, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, NO3 -, HCO3 -, SO4 2-) at a concentration of 10 mmol/L, with ce-GQDs at 0.85 μg/mL. The fluorescent intensities of ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion without and with ions or anions are expressed as F 0 and F, respectively. The photograph shows the ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion with different ions or anions under excitation. Scale bar: 2 cm. (D) Fluorescent titration of various K+ concentrations (0–125 mmol/L) in ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion. Inset: photograph of ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion with different concentrations of K+ under excitation. Scale bar: 2 cm. (E) Linear relationship derives from the fluorescent titration. (F) Fluorescent lifetimes of ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion without and with K+ (125 mmol/L).

Relaxometry sensing mechanism of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ towards K+. (A) Comparison of T 1 of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ and ce-GQDs-Gd3+ before and after binding with K+. A two-tailed Students’ T-test was used to evaluate the significance level between the two groups. Significance levels are shown as n.s.: P > 0.05; **** P < 0.0001. Error bars indicate the standard deviation (SD) of three reduplicative tests. (B) T 1 test of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ at various concentrations of K+ ranging from 0 to 150 mmol/L. Significance levels are shown as n.s.: P > 0.05; *P < 0.05; ** P < 0.01; *** P < 0.001; **** P < 0.0001. (C) Linear fit of ΔT 1 versus K+ concentration in ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ aqueous dispersion. R 2 = 0.997. (D) Anti-interference test for various ions and anions in organisms (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, NH4 +, Cu2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, NO3 -, HCO3 -, and SO4 2-) at a concentration of 10 mmol/L and K+ at a concentration of 150 mmol/L, with ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ at 0.92 μg/mL. T 1 and T 1o represent the T 1 values of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ aqueous dispersion with and without the addition of ions or anions.

Bio-toxicity and bio-applications of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+. (A) Viabilities of HUVECs co-incubated with 200 μg/mL of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ for 0–72 h, the cell viabilities of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+-treated and untreated cells are expressed in OD and OD0, respectively. (B) Viabilities of HUVECs co-incubated with different concentrations (0, 10, 25, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μg/mL) of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ for 24 h. Error bars indicate the SD of five reduplicative tests. (C) Tissue slices of various organs were harvested from nude mice after injecting ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ (200 mg/kg) via the tail vein for 24 h. Scale bar: 2 nm. (D) Bright-field and fluorescent images of healthy and senescent cells after incubation with ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ (200 μg/mL, 24 h). Scale bar: 2 nm. (E) Illustration of the tail vein injection process. (F) The concentration of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ in the cerebral spinal fluid (CSF) after injection of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ with different concentrations (0–1 mg/kg) for 12 h. (G) The concentration of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ in CSF after injection of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ (0.2 mg/kg) for different times. All the experiments were obtained via the tail vein injection in nude mice.

head.ce:title: K+ concentration-based NMR-fluorescence dual-functional senescence sensing using graphene quantum dots with crown ether structure

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Optical-Electrical and Computer Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aging allows the occurrence and progression of cerebrovascular diseases, highlighting the importance of detecting senescent cells and tissues in the nervous system. Detecting senescent cells/tissues using nanoparticles as probes in the nervous system is desired with high sensitivity, high accuracy, and penetration of the blood-brain barrier. In response, we designed crown ether-based graphene quantum dots with fast proton transport channels to sense potassium ions, a significant differential indicator between healthy and senescent cells. Using the probe, we have achieved MRI-fluorescence dual-functional monitoring of potassium ion concentration in cells via fluorescent intensity and magnetic relaxation time. Importantly, we have further demonstrated the advantages of the crown ether-based graphene quantum dots in penetrating the blood-brain barrier, revealing their potential for diagnosing aging in the nervous system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aging results from the impact of the accumulation of a wide variety of molecular and cellular damage over time, which is also a contributing factor for multiple diseases [1]. Notably, cerebrovascular diseases present health threats in aging [2]. Aging-related studies involve two critical aspects: accurate diagnosis and effective treatment [3,4], depending on pinpointing the specific areas of senescent cells or tissues. To sense aging, especially for identifying aging in cerebrovascular systems, it is crucial to improve diagnostic accuracy [5] and develop technologies that are capable of penetrating the blood-brain barrier (BBB) [6–8]. The potassium ion (K+) concentration in senescent cells is low compared to that in healthy cells (120–150 mmol/L) [9] due to impaired cell functions, which makes K+ concentration a valuable indicator to distinguish between healthy and senescent cells [10]. As cells are senescent, the reduction in intracellular K+ concentration becomes more pronounced [1], allowing for identifying cells with senescent characteristics. Detecting K+ concentration in living organisms aids in understanding the development of age-related diseases and monitoring their progression [11,12]. However, detecting and quantifying K+ is challenging due to its chemical similarity with other ions in biological systems [11]. Hence, designing a suitable structure with BBB penetration capability for sensing K+ concentration is critical for detecting K+ in living organisms [13].

Currently, fluorescent sensing [10] and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [14,15] are the primary methods for in vivo detecting K+ in biological systems. Fluorescent sensors are often based on fluorescence-quenching mechanisms [16,17]. Nevertheless, fluorescent sensors usually suffer from a small penetration depth [18]. Alternatively, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) allows direct detection of the 39K signal and imaging depending on the K+ concentration [15,19]. The main challenge of 39K MRI is the low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), attributed to the low concentration of K+ in the body [14] and its low gyromagnetic ratio of 39K (γK = 1.99 MHz/T) [15]. Therefore, developing a probe that converts the 39K signal into a 1H (γH =42.57 MHz/T) signal could address the low SNR from the 39K MRI. By combining fluorescent and NMR techniques, more comprehensive information for K+ detection can be obtained, leading to diagnostics with high accuracy [20,21].

A dual-functional probe is essential to detect K+ effectively using fluorescent and NMR techniques. The nanosized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with numerous advantages, including diversified photoluminescence, modifiable structure, low toxicity, and outstanding biocompatibility, enable them to synthesize the probe for K+ sensing [22–24]. The modifiable structure of GQDs allows them to chelate paramagnetic gadolinium ion (Gd3+) to possess para-magnetism [25,26], enabling the conversion of the 39K signal into the 1H signal. The above characters allow GQDs to serve as fluorescent and NMR dual-functional probes [27,28], highlighting their potential in K+ detection with high accuracy.

Herein, we synthesize magnetic GQDs with crown ether structure for K+ capture [29], and the PEG5 acted as a bridge molecule linking the magnetic center (Gd3+) and the sensing center (ce-GQDs), thus enabling the NMR response towards K+ [30]. We thus achieve a dual-functional probe that detects the concentration of K+ in cells through magnetic resonance relaxation and fluorescence with high sensitivity. Subsequently, we evaluated BBB penetration using the obtained GQDs, laying the groundwork for further development of diagnostic approaches for aging in the nervous systems.

Ethyl alcohol (AR), o-phenylenediamine (oPD, 99.5 %), 4’-bromobenzo-18-crown 6-ether (95 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.99 %), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 99 %), calcium chloride (CaCl2, 97 %), copper chloride (CuCl2, 99 %), iron chloride (FeCl3, 99.9 %), ferrous sulfate (FeSO4, 98 %), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 99.95 %), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl, 99.99 %), sodium nitrate -15N (NaNO3, 99.99 %), sodium sulfate (NaSO4, 99 %), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, 99.7 %), and potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5 %) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. All chemical solvents and reagents required no additional purification before use. Deionized water used throughout all experiments was obtained using a Milli-Q system with a resistivity of ∼18.2 MΩ cm at 25 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of K+ responsive crown ether-structured GQDs (ce-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The ce-GQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. 4’-bromobenzo-18-crown 6-ether and o-phenylenediamine were chosen as the molecular precursors [31]. To begin, 391.3 mg (0.1 mmol) of 4’-bromobenzo-18-crown 6-ether and 108.1 mg (0.1 mmol) of o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 15 mL of ethanol. The solution was then placed in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave, heated to 180 °C within 30 min, and maintained at 180 °C for 50 h. Once the solution was naturally cooled to room temperature, the ethanol in the solvent was replaced with deionized water through vacuum distillation at 48 °C. Subsequently, the solution was filtered using a 220 nm membrane to eliminate any aggregates. The clear yellow filtrate was then placed in a cellulose dialysis bag (100–500 Da) and dialyzed against deionized water for 48 h to remove any remaining unreacted precursors. After further distillation and filtration, the final product was an aqueous ce-GQDs dispersion. The synthesis strategy for ce-GQDs is depicted in Fig. 1 A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Gd3+ loaded PEG5 modified ce-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The process began with the preparation of PEG5-modified ce-GQDs (ce-GQDs-PEG5) via a hydrothermal reaction, combining ce-GQDs (15 mL, 0.85 mg/mL) with PEG5 (150 μL, 0.10 mol/L). This mixture was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave, heated to 140 °C within 30 min, and then kept at this temperature for 6 h to form ce-GQDs-PEG5. Following this, 300 μL of Gd(NO3)3 solution (0.05 mol/L) was added to the ce-GQDs-PEG5 aqueous dispersion and subjected to the same heating process. The product was then dialyzed using a 100–500 Da membrane against deionized water for 48 h to remove residual PEG5 and Gd(NO3)3. The final product is the ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ aqueous dispersion.

Images from transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were captured using a Hitachi H-8100 electron microscope, a product of Hitachi from Tokyo, Japan, set at 80 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) data were analyzed using a Bruker Dimension Icon system. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were taken using a Quantera II system from PHI, manufactured by Ulvac-PHI INC in Japan. The fluorescent emission and excitation spectra in liquid medium were recorded using a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer, acquired from PerkinElmer Instruments in the U.K. Additionally, the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a UV5800 spectrophotometer. Longitudinal relaxation time (T 1) values were measured with a homemade ultra-low-field (ULF) MRI scanner operating at 114 μT (unless specified otherwise) [27,32,33]. These experiments were conducted at an ambient temperature of 25 °C.

The crown ether structures were introduced into the sp
2 carbon framework of GQDs via the nucleophilic substitution reactions between 4’-bromobenzo-18-crown 6-ether and o-phenylenediamine [31]. Due to the well-known reaction between bromides and aromatic amines, the 4’-bromobenzo-18-crown 6-ether provides the crown ether structure composition. The reaction time-dependent matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOFMS) was used to demonstrate the forming process of sp
2 carbon framework with crown ether structures (ce-GQDs) [34]. As shown in Fig. S1, the nucleophilic substitution reactions between 4’-bromobenzo-18-crown 6-ether and o-phenylenediamine were demonstrated by mass spectrometry intuitively. The peaks in the MALDI-TOFMS spectrum at m/z = 416.2, 418.5, 520.2, 624.3, 830.4, 936.4, 1010.4, 1020.4, 1107.4, and 1431.8 are attributed to the protonated intermediate products with crown ether structures, respectively.

Fig. 1 B presents the TEM image of the ce-GQDs, which exhibits high dispersity. The size distribution histogram (inset of Fig. 1 B) shows that the ce-GQDs range from 1.84 to 10.12 nm in lateral size, with an average size of 3.73 nm. Such a small average size ensures optimal dispersion suitable for subsequent biological experiments [35]. After modifying with PEG5, the average size of the ce-GQDs-PEG5 increases to 4.82 nm, as indicated in Fig. S2. Such a size increase originates from the modification of PEG5 on the ce-GQDs’ surface [30]. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1 C) displays the ce-GQDs, which includes a crown ether structure in ce-GQDs-PEG5. Moreover, the HR-TEM image shows a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. This lattice can be attributed to the (110) diffraction planes of graphitic (sp
2 ) carbon. The diffraction spot (Fig. S3) analysis further confirms the hexagonal symmetry, indicating a well-defined crystal structure of the ce-GQDs [36]. Fig. 1 D displays a representative AFM characterization of highly dispersed ce-GQDs on a SiO2/Si substrate. The measured thickness of ce-GQDs ranges from 2.42 to 8.62 nm (Fig. 1 E) [34,37]. Likewise, the thickness of the ce-GQDs-PEG5 ranges from 2.83 to 8.84 nm (Fig. S4). The thickness has no significant change after surface modification.

XPS was used to characterize the chemical structure of ce-GQDs. Both the XPS survey spectra of the ce-GQDs and ce-GQDs-PEG5 show a C 1 s signal at 285 eV, along with an O 1 s signal at 531 eV and an N 1 s signal at 400 eV (Fig. S5 and S6). Initially, the relative atomic contents of C, N, and O in ce-GQDs are 61.70 at%, 3.27 at%, and 35.03 at%, respectively [27]. After PEG5 modification, the atomic contents of C, N, and O shift slightly in ce-GQDs-PEG5 to 60.63 at%, 3.81 at%, and 35.56 at%, respectively, revealing the unchanged elemental composition of ce-GQDs after the modification of PEG5. Due to the low relative content of N, the peaks of N 1 s spectra in the XPS survey are inconspicuous (Fig. S5–S7 , Table S1). The XPS peaks in the C 1 s and O 1 s spectra of ce-GQDs (Figs. 1 F and 1 G) represent various chemical bonds, C–C/CC bonds at 284.75 eV, C–O bonds at 285.69 eV, and CO bonds 288.27 eV; as well as CO bonds at 531.23 eV, C–OH/C–O–C bonds at 532.59 eV, and O–CO bonds at 535.85 eV. The introduction of PEG5 increases the relative content of C–O and CO bonds in the C 1 s of ce-GQDs-PEG5 [32], with the relative content for C–O bonds increasing from 27.27 % in ce-GQDs to 34.87 % in ce-GQDs-PEG5, and for CO bonds from 7.79 % to 13.85 %, respectively (Table S1). In the N 1 s spectra of ce-GQDs (Fig. S5), peaks at 398.19 and 399.77 eV correspond to the C–N (–NH2) and CN bonds, respectively. Indeed, a notable signal at 407.37 eV indicates the presence of NO3 −. The extended solvothermal reaction time led to partial oxidation of the NH2 groups. However, this oxidation does not compromise the material's overall performance, as it is not directly associated with the sensing performance. Additionally, the XPS profile after the introduction of Gd3+ to ce-GQDs-PEG5 (Fig. S7) shows additional peaks at 142.86 and 151.38 eV, which can be attributed to Gd 4d signal, confirming the connection of Gd3+ into the ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ structure, with a Gd3+ content of 0.76 at% [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing mechanism of ce-GQDs towards K+
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous research, the crown ether structure exhibits supramolecular binding selectivity for K+ which leading to fluorescent quenching of ce-GQDs [9,39]. Furthermore, the potential quenching mechanism may be attributed to photoinduced electron transfer and intramolecular charge transfer [40,41]. The proposed ce-GQDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method using 4’-bromobenzo-18-crown 6-ether and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. Fig. 2 A illustrates the fluorescent quenching of ce-GQDs in the presence of K+. After combining with K+, the fluorescence of ce-GQDs is quenched.

To assess the optical properties of ce-GQDs, the normalized UV–vis absorption spectrum of ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion is presented in Fig. 2 B [27]. The absorption peak at 224 nm is attributed to the π−π* transition (sp
2 domains ce-GQDs) [24]. The maximum photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and emission (PL) wavelengths of the ce-GQDs are observed at 270 nm and 370 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 B), indicating the blue-emitting property of ce-GQDs. To evaluate the effects of ions and anions that exist in biological systems on the fluorescent intensity of ce-GQDs, fluorescent measurements were conducted with the presence of different ions or anions (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, NH4 +, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, NO3 -, HCO3 -, and SO4 2-). The fluorescent intensities are plotted in Fig. 2 C and expressed as F/F 0. Fig. 2 C reveals no significant decreases in fluorescent intensity when the above-mentioned ions or anions were present, thereby indicating that these ions and anions have negligible impact on fluorescent quenching of ce-GQDs compared to K+.

To quantify the influence of K+ on the fluorescent intensity of ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion, the fluorescent emission spectra of ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion with different concentrations of K+ (0–125 mmol/L) were recorded (Fig. 2 D). As the K+ concentration increases from 2.5 mmol/L to 25 mmol/L, a notable decrease in fluorescent intensity has been recorded. A linear fit was used to quantify the relationship between K+ concentration and the fluorescent intensity of ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion (Fig. 2 E), demonstrating a linear correlation (R 2 = 0.99) over the concentration range of 2.5–25 mmol/L. The fluorescence quenching data in Fig. 2 E are described by the equation:
F
0
F
= 1 + K [ Q ]
where [Q] represents the concentration of the analyte (K+), and
F
0
and F are PL intensities of ce-CQDs in the absence and presence of K+, respectively.

The limit of detection (LOD) for K+ can be thus calculated to be 3.80 mmol/L [42]. Additionally, the indistinctive change in fluorescent lifetimes of ce-GQDs aqueous dispersion before and after the interaction with K+ (from 3.49 ns to 3.48 ns, Fig. 2 F) indicates the reduction in fluorescent intensity is static quenching [30].

Notably, the crown ether structure in ce-GQDs is tightly integrated into the sp 2-sp 3 hybrid carbon nanostructure of GQDs as an integral unit. This compact structure prevents the structure of ce-GQDs from being damaged by further modifications. Therefore, the further structural modifications of ce-GQDs to enable magnetic relaxation response, do not affect their fluorescent response properties, with ce-GQDs being the core component for K+ detection. Indeed, after further modification to form ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+, the resulting composite remains the same fluorescent response properties as ce-GQDs. Based on this, the LOD for K+ of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ also can be thus calculated to be 3.80 mmol/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relaxometry sensing mechanism of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ towards K+
ce:section.ce:para: Integrating fluorescent and NMR techniques could enhance the detection accuracy of K+ in diagnostic applications [20,21,43,44]. Since T 1 provides a primary source for image contrast in MRI, it can be adopted to assess the relaxometry response of ce-GQDs towards K+. As plotted in Fig. 3 A, when ce-GQDs was directly chelated with Gd3+ (ce-GQDs-Gd3+), T 1 of the ce-GQDs-Gd3+ aqueous dispersion remained unchanged without and with the presence of K+ (150 mmol/L). Such a result indicates that ce-GQDs-Gd3+ has no relaxometry response towards K+. Fortunately, when ce-GQDs were modified with PEG5 and subsequently chelated with Gd3+ to form ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ (Fig. S8), the resulting ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ demonstrated a significant relaxometry response towards K+, which was evidenced by the T 1 measurement of the ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ aqueous dispersion. The T 1 of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ (0.92 μg/mL) decreases from 512.82 ms to 169.29 ms as K+ concentrations increase from 0 to 150 mmol/L (Fig. 3 A, Fig. S9).

To further explore the relaxometry sensing mechanism of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+, K+ titration experiments were carried out. A decreased trend in T 1 of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ aqueous dispersion with the increased K+ concentrations from 5 to 150 mmol/L can be observed (Fig. 3 B). Additionally, a linear relationship between the ΔT 1 (ΔT 1 is defined as T 1 of the ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ aqueous dispersion minus T 1 of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ aqueous dispersion with the presence of ions or anions) and K+ concentration can be found in Fig. 3 C. The LOD for ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ in K+ sensing has been calculated as 14.12 mmol/L, which indicates that the ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ can sense K+ through T 1 measurement. Such a T 1 response can be attributed to the change in proton concentration around the para-magnetic Gd3+, which is brought by ce-GQDs in ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ through the fast proton transport channel provided by the PEG5 chain. The Gd3+ is linked to PEG5, which serves as a bridging molecule between the magnetic center Gd3+ and the sensing center (ce-GQDs). The low energy barrier in the PEG5 tube leads to the fast proton transport between ce-GQDs and Gd3+ [30].

To evaluate the effects of ions and anions that exist in biological systems on T 1 of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+, anti-interference was tested for various ions and anions that exist in organisms (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, NH4 +, Cu2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, NO3 -, HCO3 -, and SO4 2-). T 1 and T 1o represent the T 1 values of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ aqueous dispersion with and without the addition of ions or anions. Fig. 3 D reveals no significant decreases in T 1 when these ions or anions are present, indicating that these ions and anions have negligible impact on T 1 of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ compared to K+. Additionally, Fig. S10 showed a similar fluorescent response to K+ of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ compared with ce-GQDs. A linear fit was used to quantify the relationship between K+ concentration and the fluorescent intensity of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ aqueous dispersion, demonstrating a linear correlation (R 2 = 0.99) over the concentration range of 2.5–25 mmol/L. The linearity range is consistent with ce-GQDs. The indistinctive change in fluorescent lifetimes of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ aqueous dispersion before and after the interaction with K+ (from 4.85 ns to 4.84 ns, Fig. S11) indicates the reduction in fluorescent intensity has a static quenching mechanism similar to that of ce-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological application of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: As the ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ exhibits both optical and relaxometry responses toward K+, bio-compatibility evaluations were carried out both in vitro and in vivo before performing its biological applications. As shown in Fig. 4 A, human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were incubated with 200 μg/mL of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ for different incubation times. The OD/OD0 values of HUVECs are 1.04, 0.99, 1.01, 1.02, 0.98, and 0.99 after incubated with ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ (200 μg/mL) for 12, 24, 36, 48, 60, and 72 h, respectively, indicating no significant reduction in cell viability after 72 h of incubation. Further, the effect of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ concentration on the bio-toxicity of HUVECs was assessed by incubating HUVECs with ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ (0–200 μg/mL) for 24 h (Fig. 4 B). The resulting OD/OD0 values for concentrations of 10, 25, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μg/mL are 0.99, 1.03, 1.02, 0.99, 0.99, and 0.98, respectively. No noticeable reduction in cell viability can be observed from the above experiment, confirming the low toxicity of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ in vitro.

Importantly, in vivo toxicity of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ has been evaluated using tissue slices harvested from nude mice that had been injected with ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ (Fig. 4 C). The analysis of these tissue slices reveals no significant occurrences of cell necrosis, tissue inflammation, or apoptosis within the heart, lungs, liver, kidneys, and spleen of nude mice after injection of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+, implying that ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ has negligible side effects on these tissues.

As ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ shows insignificant toxicity on cells and tissues, it can be thus applied in differentiating healthy and senescent cells based on the difference in K+ concentration. As depicted in Fig. 4 D, senescent cells exhibit different morphology compared to healthy cells. After incubation with ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+, healthy cells demonstrated a lower fluorescent intensity compared to that of senescent cells. Such a finding is consistent with the results in Fig. S10.

To sense aging, especially in cerebrovascular systems, it is significant to endow probes with the capacity to penetrate the BBB [6–8] . The BBB is a crucial, highly selective semipermeable membrane within the central nervous system (CNS) [45–47]. BBB protects the CNS by separating the bloodstream from the brain’s extracellular fluid, thereby preventing toxins and pathogens in the blood from entering the brain [6,7,46]. Additionally, it should be noted that BBB also restricts the access of most small-molecule drugs to the brain [6,48,49]. Due to the introduction of PEG5, ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ has been endowed with the ability to penetrate BBB, offering a potential method for early disease intervention.

The ability of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ to penetrate BBB was evaluated using tail vein injection (Fig. 4 E). As illustrated in Fig. 4 F, following a 12 h post-injection period, the concentration of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) increased proportionally along with the injected doses, indicating that ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ can cross the BBB without compromising its integrity even at high concentrations (1 mg/kg) [50]. However, the prolonged presence of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ in the BBB could potentially cause cellular damage and disrupt the BBB [50]. When the injection concentration of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ was 200 μg/mL, the highest concentration of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ in CSF was observed around 18 h post-injection, declining to nearly zero by 36 h (Fig. 4 G). Such a result proves the ability of ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ to penetrate the BBB without damaging its permeability.

In conclusion, we have designed a method for fluorescent sensing of aging-related K+ in the bio-system by integrating the crown ether structure into GQDs. To enable the magnetic relaxation response towards K+, we have connected ce-GQDs and the para-magnetic Gd3+ using PEG5, which facilitates the fast proton transport around Gd3+. The obtained ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ exhibits both fluorescent and magnetic relaxation responses towards K+ with high sensitivity (LOD of 3.80 mmol/L using fluorescent detection, LOD of 14.12 mmol/L using relaxometry detection) and specificity. The in vitro experiments have confirmed that ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ can effectively detect K+ and differentiate senescent and healthy cells. More importantly, ce-GQDs-PEG5-Gd3+ is capable of crossing the BBB, which will allow for the non-invasive monitoring of K+ levels in the nervous system in the future.

Chen Xiaojing: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Tao Quan: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Yang Siwei: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Wang Hang: Validation, Investigation. Wang Xuelian: Resources. Ding Guqiao: Supervision. Gao Xiumin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. dong hui: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. rong liangliang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Dr. Yongqiang Li from SIMIT for helpful discussion. All animal experiments were conducted following protocols approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the Xinhua Hospital Ethics Committee affiliated to Shanghai Jiaotong University School of Medicine. This work was financially supported by International Partnership Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (321GJHZ2023246GC), Autonomous Deployment Project of National Key Laboratory of Materials for Integrated Circuits (NKLJC-Z2023-B03), Chinese Natural Science Foundation (82100426). Prof. Siwei Yang acknowledges the support from the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of Chinese Academy of Sciences, and the Xinweizhixing Project of Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2025.137442.

Adv. Drug Deliv. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Pandit, L. Chen, J. Götz, The blood-brain barrier: Physiology and strategies for drug delivery, Adv. Drug Deliv. Rev. 165–166 (2020) 1–14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: From blood–brain barrier to blood–brain interface: new opportunities for CNS drug delivery
Nat. Rev. Drug Discov.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.A. Banks, From blood–brain barrier to blood–brain interface: new opportunities for CNS drug delivery, Nat. Rev. Drug Discov. 15 (2016) 275–292.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cell-based in vitro blood–brain barrier model can rapidly evaluate nanoparticles’ brain permeability in association with particle size and surface modification
Int. J. Mol. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Hanada, K. Fujioka, Y. Inoue, F. Kanaya, Y. Manome, K. Yamamoto, Cell-based in vitro blood–brain barrier model can rapidly evaluate nanoparticles’ brain permeability in association with particle size and surface modification, Int. J. Mol. Sci. 15 (2014) 1812–1825.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaojing Chen is a master student at University of Shanghai for Science and Technology. Her research interests include the design, preparation, and applications of fluorescent nanomaterials.

Siwei Yang works as a professor at Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He received his Ph.D. from University of Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2017. Dr. Siwei Yang first prepared a new 2D carbon-based semiconductor C3N in 2017. His research interests include controllable preparation, application (nanoelectronics, nanophotonics, and nanomedicine) of 2D carbon-based semiconductors, graphene quantum dots, and other graphene derivatives.

Xiumin Gao is a professor at University of Shanghai for Science and Technology. He received his PhD degree from Shanghai Institute of Optics and Fine Mechanics (SIOM) in 2006, and joined University of Shanghai for Science and Technology （USST） in 2017. He is leader of several research Programs including National Natural Science Foundation of China，National key research and development plan. He has published more than 60 papers in journals such as Nature Communications, Scientific Reports, and Applied Optics. He obtained more than 50 patents, and his several research achievements have been industrialized. His current research includes instruments and meters, smart sensing technology, optical engineering, and precision measurement.

Hui Dong is now a professor at Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology, Chinese Academy of Science. She received her Ph.D. from University of Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2011. She worked as visiting scholar in Forschungszentrum Jülich (20082010) and the University of California at Berkeley (20132014). Prof. Dr. Hui Dong mainly works on the development of superconducting quantum interface device-based ultra-low field NMR/MRI system and its applications in biosensing/bioimaging and spectroscopic analysis.
